Calibrating feature weights

Source: vignettes/calibrating-weights.Rmd
calibrating-weights.Rmd

Why calibrate?

ELSA prioritizations maximise a weighted, additive utility: the total amount of each feature secured, summed across planning units and action zones. Because the objective is additive, spatially extensive features — and features that co-occur with areas that are valuable to other features — are favoured over rarer or more isolated features when every feature is weighted equally. Some features end up well represented while others are left far below what they could achieve.

elsar_calibrate_weights() computes a per-feature weight w_j that counteracts this, so features achieve more even representation relative to their own single-feature maximum. It reproduces the ELSA calibration framework exactly; this vignette shows how to run it and read the result.

How it works

  1. Single-feature maxima. Each feature j is maximised on its own (weight 1 for j, 0 for the rest) to find its maximum attainable representation a_j^max.
  2. Baseline. The problem is solved with equal weights to get each feature’s representation a_j and relative shortfall delta_j = (a_j - a_j^max) / a_j^max.
  3. Iterate. Weights are nudged up — most for the worst-represented feature, none for the best — the problem is re-solved, and shortfalls recomputed. This repeats until the allocation stops changing; the weights giving the most even representation (smallest spread of delta_j) are returned.

The objective and update rule are unchanged from the original ELSA tool; the implementation additionally returns the best-seen weights, stops once the allocation freezes, and excludes zero-total features.

A worked example

We build a small synthetic stack of four features with different spatial footprints, two action zones, and per-zone impacts and budgets.

library(elsar)
library(terra)
#> terra 1.9.34

set.seed(1)
r0 <- rast(nrows = 24, ncols = 24, xmin = 0, xmax = 24, ymin = 0, ymax = 24,
           crs = "EPSG:3857")
xx <- init(r0, "x")
yy <- init(r0, "y")

features <- c(
  init(r0, runif),                          # broad, diffuse
  (xx < 8) * 1,                             # concentrated strip
  ((xx - 12)^2 + (yy - 12)^2 < 40) * 1,     # central disc
  (yy > 20) * init(r0, runif)               # sparse band
)
names(features) <- c("diffuse", "strip", "disc", "band")

pu_all   <- ifel(!is.na(features[[1]]), 1, NA)
zones_pu <- list(protect = pu_all, restore = pu_all)

impacts <- data.frame(
  feature = names(features),
  protect = c(1.0, 1.0, 1.0, 1.0),
  restore = c(0.6, 0.3, 0.8, 0.5)
)
budgets <- c(protect = 20, restore = 15)  # % of planning-unit area

Run the calibration. We use the free HiGHS solver here; in production you would typically use solver = "gurobi".

cal <- elsar_calibrate_weights(
  features       = features,
  impacts        = impacts,
  zones_pu       = zones_pu,
  planning_units = pu_all,
  budgets        = budgets,
  solver         = "highs",
  verbose        = FALSE
)
#> [2026-07-10 08:51:24] Calculated target: 115 (20% of total PU area)
#> [2026-07-10 08:51:24] Calculated target: 86 (15% of total PU area)

The calibrated weights:

cal$weights
#> # A tibble: 4 × 2
#>   feature weight
#>   <chr>    <dbl>
#> 1 diffuse   2.10
#> 2 strip     7.46
#> 3 disc      7.17
#> 4 band     11.0

The representation profile shows how close each feature gets to its single-feature maximum (ratio = a / a_max); calibration lifts the weakest features:

cal$representation
#> # A tibble: 4 × 5
#>   feature     a a_max ratio delta_pct
#>   <chr>   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>     <dbl>
#> 1 diffuse 0.367 0.490 0.748     -25.2
#> 2 strip   0.5   0.719 0.695     -30.5
#> 3 disc    0.690 0.992 0.696     -30.4
#> 4 band    0.674 1     0.674     -32.6

The per-iteration trajectory records how the spread of shortfalls shrinks and when the allocation froze:

cal$trajectory[, c("iter", "spread_new", "max_abs_util_change", "weight_max")]
#> # A tibble: 8 × 4
#>    iter spread_new max_abs_util_change weight_max
#>   <int>      <dbl>               <dbl>      <dbl>
#> 1     0      33.4             NA             1   
#> 2     1      35.2              0.372         3   
#> 3     2      30.7              0.302         3.02
#> 4     3       9.43             0.163         5.02
#> 5     4       9.67             0.0167        7.02
#> 6     5       8.92             0.00334       9.02
#> 7     6       7.41             0.00565      11.0 
#> 8     7       7.41             0            13.0
cal$converged
#> [1] TRUE
cal$iterations
#> [1] 7

Using the weights

At run time the ELSA tool multiplies these calibration weights by any user-specified weights, so w_j = w_j^user * w_j^cal (with w_j^user = 1 by default). Persist cal$weights alongside your prepared layers and load them as the weight_calibration for each feature.

Solvers

elsar_calibrate_weights() needs prioritizr and a supported solver:

  • solver = "gurobi" — fastest; requires the commercial gurobi R package.
  • solver = "highs" — free and open-source (install.packages("highs")).
  • solver = "default" — prioritizr’s best available solver.

Transient solver or cloud-connection failures are retried automatically (solve_retries).

On this page
